mappings.rationaleAI: Neoplatonism: philosophy of Neoplatonists, Jewish/Christian philosophy.
Aquinas: age of Schoolmen, reconciling philosophy and theology.
Roger Bacon: new philosophical styles vying with Thomism.
Happiness: Plato, Aristotle, Stoics, Epicureans.
Materialism: Democritus through Hobbes to Marx.
Pythagoras: metaphysics of number.
Plato's Gorgias: Socratic dialectic, ethics.
Spinoza: speculative Rationalist tradition.
Epicureanism.
Maimonides: Arabic and Jewish philosophy.
Warring States: classical Chinese philosophy.
Empiricism: Locke, Hume.
Stoicism.
12th-century: Arabic Aristotle translations to West.
Translation Movement: Islamic philosophers preserving Aristotle.
Scientific Method: Bacon, Popper.
Baconian Science: empiricist tradition.
Heraclitus: flux, logos, metaphysics of matter.
Plato's Symposium: Forms, desire, beauty.
Plato's Atlantis: politics, metaphysics.
Pragmatism: James, Dewey.
Scepticism: Pyrrhonian, Descartes, Hume.
Socrates: examined life.
School of Athens: Renaissance renewal of Plato.
Al-Ghazali: Islamic philosophy.
Al-Kindi: Islamic/Aristotelian philosophy.
Aristotle's Politics.
Socrates: dialectical method.
Ockham: nominalism, late medieval philosophy.
Avicenna: Islamic philosophy.
Hobbes: political theory, empiricist tradition.
Social Contract: Hobbes through Rousseau.
Kant's Copernican Revolution.
Upanishads: presystematic Indian philosophy.
Han Synthesis: Confucianism, Neo-Confucianism.
Karma: Indian metaphysics, ethics.
Age of Doubt: Hegel, Nietzsche.
Sovereignty: Bodin, Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau.
Abbasid: Islamic philosophers, Aristotle transmission.
Just War: Augustine, Aquinas.
Good and Evil: Nietzsche.
Laws of Nature: Thales to philosophy of science.
Alcuin's promotion of the liberal arts and Anglo-Saxon humanism relates directly to philosophy and the liberal arts in the schools of the Christian West from the 9th to 11th century (A.2.a.ii).

The ethical debate about animal consciousness and moral status connects to the 19th-century reaction against Rationalism (A.3.d.iii), particularly regarding moral philosophy and the challenge to purely mechanistic views of non-human beings.
The Nicomachean Ethics is Aristotle's central work on ethics, politics, and the good life, directly covered under Aristotle's teleological positions in ethics and politics (A.1.b.iii).

Nietzsche's prediction that morality would perish connects to the 19th-century reaction against Rationalism (A.3.d.iii Nietzsche), and the episode explores philosophical questions about the psychology of evil.

The episode traces authenticity from Aristotle (A.1.b.iii), through St Augustine (A.2.a.i), to Kierkegaard and Nietzsche (A.3.d.iii reaction against Rationalism), and Heidegger and Sartre in 20th-century existentialism (A.3.e 20th-century philosophy).
Bergson's intuitionism and philosophy of time are explicitly covered as an independent speculative philosophy of the 20th century (A.3.e.i Bergson's intuitionism).

Berkeley's immaterialism -- that objects only exist when perceived -- and his influence on Hume and Kant are directly covered under epistemological issues of the Enlightenment, specifically Locke and Berkeley's inquiry into the origin and nature of reason (A.3.c.i).
Camus's philosophical exploration of absurdity and existence, alongside his friendship and falling-out with Sartre, places him in 20th-century philosophy (A.3.e), particularly existentialism.

The episode traces civility through Enlightenment thinkers including Locke, Hume, and Kant on epistemology and social philosophy (A.3.c.i, A.3.c.iii), and post-Reformation philosophical contributions in the Empiricist and Rationalist traditions (A.3.b.ii).

The episode traces common sense philosophy through the Empiricist tradition of Locke, Hume, and Reid (A.3.b.i), and the epistemological issues of the Enlightenment including Hume's science of man and Kant's critical examination of reason (A.3.c.i).

Cynicism originated with Diogenes as a post-Socratic philosophical movement rooted in Socratic ethical concerns (A.1.b.i), and flourished during the Hellenistic period alongside Stoicism and Epicureanism (A.1.c).

Elizabeth Anscombe was a leading figure in 20th-century analytic philosophy, contributing to independent speculative and social philosophies alongside Wittgenstein-influenced linguistic analysis (A.3.e.i).

Arendt's political philosophy drew on Greek philosophy and existentialist thought, connecting to 20th-century independent speculative and social philosophies (A.3.e.i) and the reaction against Rationalism including existentialist currents (A.3.d.iii).
The discussion of heroic virtue, honor, and community values connects to the Sophists' anthropological turn (A.1.a.iv) and the ethical concerns of Socrates and Plato on virtue (A.1.b).
Horace's Epicurean philosophy connects to the philosophy of the Epicureans in Hellenistic and Roman thought (A.1.c.ii).

Murdoch's moral philosophy challenged existentialist subjectivism and emotivism, connecting to 20th-century independent speculative philosophies (A.3.e.i), and drew on Plato (A.1.b.ii) to assert objective moral goodness.

The Vienna Circle members (Schlick, Carnap, Neurath) and their dispersal to Britain and America profoundly influenced 20th-century analytic philosophy, connecting to independent speculative and social philosophies of the 20th century (A.3.e.i).

Astell's argument turning Lockean liberal theory against patriarchy connects to social and political philosophy of the Enlightenment, specifically the concern of Locke with freedom and equality (A.3.c.iii).

Wollstonecraft's Vindication of the Rights of Woman applied Enlightenment principles of reason and natural rights to women's equality, directly engaging the social and political philosophy of Locke and Rousseau (A.3.c.iii).

Mill's utilitarianism, empiricism, and political philosophy are directly covered under 19th-century developments in the empirical and scientific tradition, including Comte's Positivism, Mill's theory of knowledge and ethics (A.3.d.ii).

Burke and Kant's theories of the sublime connect to Enlightenment epistemological issues (A.3.c.i) including Kant's critical examination of reason, and the resurgence of metaphysical spirit in Idealism (A.3.d.i).
Romanticism's philosophical dimensions connect to the resurgence of metaphysical spirit and Idealism (A.3.d.i) and the reaction against Rationalism including Kierkegaard and Schopenhauer (A.3.d.iii).
The philosophical questions about truth, authenticity, and the relationship between fact and fiction connect to 20th-century philosophical concerns with epistemology and the philosophy of language.

Hazlitt's early philosophical work on the principles of human action, arguing that imagination enables transcendence of self-interest, connects to early 19th-century philosophy and the idealist tradition (A.3.d.i).

Nietzsche, Wells, and the philosophical critique of idealism and social engineering connect to 19th- and 20th-century Continental philosophy (A.4.b) and contemporary philosophy including political philosophy (A.4.c).

Nietzsche's moral philosophy is a central topic in 19th-century Continental philosophy (A.4.b); his engagement with Stoic ethics and the master-slave distinction echoes Hellenistic philosophical traditions (A.1.c.i).
Seneca as a major Stoic philosopher emphasizing virtue, self-control, and acceptance of fate, elaborating Stoic thought during the late Roman Republic and empire (A.1.c.i).

Paine translated Enlightenment political philosophy including social contract theory and natural rights into accessible prose, connecting to Enlightenment rationalism (A.3.b) and 18th-century British empiricism and political philosophy (A.3.c).
Aristotle's definition of tragedy in the Poetics as a foundational philosophical contribution to aesthetics and literary theory (A.1.b.iii).

Hume's 18th-century empiricist critique of miracles as violations of natural law is a landmark in British empiricist philosophy (A.3.c); Augustine's 4th-century theological defense connects to medieval Christian philosophy (A.2.b).
De Gouges's rights theory emerges from Enlightenment political philosophy concerning natural law, social contract, and universal rights (A.3.b).

Rawls's A Theory of Justice as the most influential work of 20th-century political philosophy, challenging utilitarianism through the original position and veil of ignorance, is a central topic in contemporary Anglo-American philosophy (A.4.c).
Philosophical roots in Shaftesbury's and Hume's moral sentiment theory connect to Enlightenment British philosophy (A.3.b, A.3.c).
De Beauvoir's existentialist philosophy, her partnership with Sartre, and The Second Sex as a foundational feminist philosophical text are central to 20th-century Continental philosophy (A.4.c).

The dissolution of medieval faith and emergence of the autonomous individual connects to Renaissance philosophy's recovery of classical thought (A.2.c) and the transition to early modern philosophy (A.3.a).
